blockers.section: API Endpoints ‚Äî POST /api/tasks/:id/complete, POST /api/tasks/:id/fail; ADR-005 Synthetic terminal runs; Implementation Design ‚Äî OrchestrationManager.CompleteTask/FailTask
blockers.issue: The new task-level terminal operator path (`CompleteTask`/`FailTask`) is underspecified for the case where a token-fenced active run exists. The spec only says it must "preserve protected terminal mutation behavior" ‚Äî but no such behavior exists today (this is a brand-new endpoint), and the spec gives no rule for what happens when an in-flight worker holds a valid claim_token, is heart-beating, and an operator forces a task-level terminal write. Possible behaviors (reject, force-stop session then write terminal, race with a concurrent `CompleteRunLease`, double terminal write, leak `current_run_id`) are all observably different and all left to interpretation.
blockers.rationale: L-005 (authoritative-primitive exclusivity) and the project's safety posture require terminal mutations to have one authoritative path. A vague "preserve protected behavior" clause for a non-existent prior behavior invites two implementations diverging across HTTP, UDS, and contract tests. Without a deterministic rule the cy-create-tasks consumer cannot generate executable tests for the protected path.
blockers.suggested_fix: Add an explicit subsection "Task-level terminal mutation against an active run" enumerating: (a) whether `CompleteTask`/`FailTask` rejects when a non-terminal run exists with a held claim_token, or (b) requests `SessionExecutor.RequestTaskStop`+grace+`ForceTaskStop` then writes the terminal transition through the same `task.Service` path that `FailRunLease` uses, including idempotency guard against a concurrent agent `CompleteRunLease`. Specify the precedence order, the actor classification (operator-only vs agent-allowed), and the resulting `current_run_id`/event sequence.

blockers.section: Data Models ‚Äî `tasks` synthetic terminal runs; ADR-005 transition matrix item 7
blockers.issue: Synthetic-terminal-run creation has no concurrency guard. Two concurrent `CompleteTask` (or `CompleteTask`+`FailTask`) calls against the same never-claimed task can each observe "no active non-terminal run" and each insert a synthetic `task_runs` row with the next free attempt number, then race on `tasks.current_run_id` set/clear and on the task-level terminal write. The spec mandates same-transaction set+clear of the projection but does not specify the uniqueness/serialization primitive that prevents the duplicate synthetic row, the duplicate terminal task event, or the attempt-counter race.
blockers.rationale: L-003 requires `task_runs` to be the single authoritative queue; producing two synthetic terminal runs for one operator intent corrupts attempt history and creates two `task.run_summary_recorded` events for one logical close. The greenfield rule does not excuse silent races ‚Äî it requires the writer authority to serialize.
blockers.suggested_fix: Specify the concurrency contract: synthetic-run creation runs inside `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` with a guard `WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM task_runs WHERE task_id = ? AND status NOT IN ('completed','failed','canceled'))` and a CAS on `tasks.status` (terminal-target). Also state idempotency: a second synthetic-creation call against an already-terminal task MUST short-circuit and return the existing terminal task/run without writing a second event.

blockers.section: Data Models ‚Äî `tasks` spawn failure policy item 4 ("`ClaimNextRun` must not return runs for tasks whose spawn circuit is open")
blockers.issue: The spec asserts that the authoritative claim primitive must filter out tasks whose `spawn_failure_count >= spawn_failure_limit` (or whose status moved to `blocked`), but provides no SQL/index strategy. Today `ClaimNextRun` is the load-bearing exclusivity primitive (L-003/L-005) backed by `idx_task_runs_pending_claim` (status, lease_until, queued_at, id) which has no task-level columns. Adding a JOIN against `tasks` and a filter on `spawn_failure_count`/`status='blocked'` changes the locking surface, plan shape, and recovery semantics of the most safety-critical query in the system without a design.
blockers.rationale: Authoritative-primitive exclusivity (L-005) means changes to `ClaimNextRun` must be designed up front, not improvised at task time. A wrong index choice can degrade the claim path under load or produce stale reads of `spawn_failure_count` racing with `IncrementSpawnFailure` writes ‚Äî both of which violate the single-queue invariant.
blockers.suggested_fix: Add an explicit "ClaimNextRun filter design" subsection specifying: the exact filter (status check on `tasks` AND/OR `spawn_failure_count >= limit`), the new/updated covering index, the transactional ordering relative to `IncrementSpawnFailure` (does the breaker open atomically with the failure increment, or is it gated by a separate task-status transition?), and contract tests proving (i) a circuit-opened task is not returned, (ii) the breaker reset on successful claim is durable in the same transaction.

blockers.section: Implementation Design ‚Äî Notification cursor primitive; ADR-003 Open Questions; Extensibility section ("No bridge SDK wire change is required unless bridge delivery consumers need cursor status exposure")
blockers.issue: `internal/notifications` ships a brand-new shared primitive (table, migration, CursorStore, hooks, observe events, four invariants) but the techspec does not migrate a single existing consumer to use it. Bridge delivery, thread notifier, and SSE replay are all named as future tenants but no MVP consumer is enumerated. The result is a half-finished surface: schema + API + tests + docs land with zero production callers, which is exactly the partial-surface completion the architecture rules forbid.
blockers.rationale: CLAUDE.md "No partial-surface completions" + L-006 (greenfield delete-not-adapt). A primitive without a consumer cannot prove monotonic-advance / at-least-once / idempotent-replay invariants under realistic load and ships dead code into a greenfield-alpha tree. ADR-003's own "Risks" section calls out exactly this failure mode.
blockers.suggested_fix: Either (a) name at least one MVP consumer migration in the same TechSpec ‚Äî e.g., `internal/bridges` thread/peer notifier moves its existing per-route advancement onto `CursorStore` with the cursor state replacing `bridge_routes.last_activity_at`-style ad hoc tracking ‚Äî and add a fresh-DB+reopen integration test for that consumer, or (b) defer the entire `internal/notifications` package to a follow-up TechSpec that lands the consumer alongside the primitive. Do not ship a primitive without a real caller.

blockers.section: Implementation Design ‚Äî Core Interfaces (OrchestrationManager.IncrementSpawnFailure / ResetSpawnFailure); Spawn failure policy item 5 ("Scheduler/session code observes spawn outcomes and calls task-service methods")
blockers.issue: The spec says "task-service-owned counter; scheduler/session code observes spawn outcomes and calls task-service methods" without specifying which package observes which spawn outcome, what the failure-classification source of truth is (mapping arbitrary spawn errors to `spawn_failed` / `session_unreachable` / `provider_auth`), or where the `Reset` hook fires ("first successful claim/session attachment" ‚Äî but claim and session attachment are distinct events with different actors). This is the safety-critical integration point that decides whether the breaker fires correctly.
blockers.rationale: L-005 requires the call-site that owns the transition to dispatch hooks ‚Äî but the techspec must name that call-site. "Scheduler/session code" is not a call-site; it is two different packages with different lifetimes and concurrency guarantees. Ambiguity here will surface as flapping breakers, lost increments, or breakers that never reset.
blockers.suggested_fix: Specify exactly one observer per outcome class: e.g., `internal/session.Manager` (or coordinator runtime) is the source of truth for spawn classification, calls `task.Service.IncrementSpawnFailure(ctx, SpawnFailure{Reason: ...})` synchronously after spawn-failure classification, and calls `task.Service.ResetSpawnFailure(ctx, SpawnFailureReset{...})` from the `AttachRunSession` path (or the first successful `StartRun`). State precisely which one resets and why claim does not (claim alone may still spawn-fail). Add an integration test for the full flap-then-recover sequence.

blockers.section: Implementation Design ‚Äî Core Interfaces (OrchestrationManager.MaintainCurrentRunID); ADR-005 invariant
blockers.issue: `MaintainCurrentRunID(ctx, mutation, actor)` is exposed as a first-class method on the orchestration interface, with set/clear actions and an `ExpectedRunID` field. Even with the rule "only task-service-owned transition methods may call it", placing it on a public interface contract is the exact misuse-vector ADR-005 warns against ‚Äî any future caller (including coordinator, scheduler, extension) can call `MaintainCurrentRunID` and accidentally treat the projection as authority.
blockers.rationale: ADR-005 is explicit: `current_run_id` is a read projection, never authority. Public surface area for projection mutation is indistinguishable from authority surface area; the invariant is then enforced only by review discipline, which lessons (L-005, L-012) say is insufficient.
blockers.suggested_fix: Remove `MaintainCurrentRunID` from the OrchestrationManager interface. Make set/clear unexported helpers callable only from inside the existing transition methods (`ClaimNextRun`, `StartRun`, `AttachRunSession`, `CompleteRunLease`, `FailRunLease`, `ReleaseRunLease`, `ReleaseSessionRunLeases`, `RecoverExpiredRunLeases`, synthetic-terminal creation, cancel/archive/delete). Add a boundary test in `internal/task` that fails if the helper is referenced from any non-task-service file.

blockers.section: API Endpoints ‚Äî POST /api/tasks/:id/complete, POST /api/tasks/:id/fail; CLI, Tools, and Agent Surfaces
blockers.issue: Authorization for the new task-level terminal endpoints is not specified. The spec says agent paths (`POST /api/agent/tasks/:run_id/complete`) resolve through session-bound lookup, but the new task-level operator path (`/api/tasks/:id/complete`) is not labeled operator-only versus agent-allowed. Any agent able to hit `/api/tasks/:id/complete` could close a task it doesn't own ‚Äî bypassing claim_token / lease semantics that protect the run-level path.
blockers.rationale: Repo-rule: agent-manageable-by-default + claim_token-secrecy + session-bound lease lookup. If the operator task-level endpoint is also reachable from agent UDS without explicit authorization rules, it becomes a backdoor around claim-token enforcement (L-005). The same applies to `task_complete-task` CLI verb (which side mounts that?).
blockers.suggested_fix: State explicitly that `POST /api/tasks/:id/complete` and `/fail` are operator-only paths (HTTP + operator UDS), not exposed under `/agent/*` or in the autonomy toolset; document the authorization boundary in the techspec, the OpenAPI spec, and the CLI verb description; add a contract test that a session-actor calling the task-level path receives the foreign-mutation error.

nits.issue: Treating `OrchestrationManager` as a separate interface alongside the existing `task.Manager` invites two consumers wiring the same underlying `task.Service` twice.
nits.suggested_fix: Either fold these methods directly into the existing `task.Manager`, or state explicitly that `OrchestrationManager` is a logical decomposition implemented by `task.Service` and consumed only by named callers (coordinator runtime, scheduler health sink) ‚Äî and that no new struct/wiring is added at composition root.

nits.section: Cursor advancement invariants ‚Äî "unless the payload is an idempotent replay of the same confirmed delivery metadata"
nits.suggested_fix: Specify the replay key explicitly: `Advance` accepts a non-strictly-increasing `last_sequence` if and only if `delivery_id` matches the stored `last_delivery_id` for that cursor row; otherwise return `ErrNonMonotonicCursor`. Add `last_delivery_id` to the `notification_cursors` schema.

nits.issue: Dispatch column says "`internal/scheduler` through read-only health telemetry" but doesn't disambiguate whether these are typed `internal/observe` events or `internal/hooks` taxonomy entries.
nits.suggested_fix: State explicitly that scheduler-side observability is `internal/observe` typed events only; no `scheduler.*` hook taxonomy is introduced (consistent with L-005 anti-pattern guidance).

nits.issue: Required Action lists "Add parity tests for HTTP/UDS" but the test plan does not enumerate which endpoints get parity tests.
nits.suggested_fix: List the parity-test matrix explicitly: every new/modified endpoint (`/api/tasks/:id/complete|fail`, `/api/task-runs/:id/complete|fail`, `/api/agent/tasks/:run_id/complete|fail`, `/api/agent/context`, `/api/tasks/dashboard`) requires an HTTP-vs-UDS shape+behavior test.

nits.section: CLI, Tools, and Agent Surfaces ‚Äî `agh task complete-task` / `agh task fail-task`
nits.issue: Hyphenated verbs are uncommon for cobra subcommands and risk drifting from existing `agh task complete <run-id>` mental model.
nits.suggested_fix: Consider `agh task close --status=completed|failed <task-id>` or a `task-level` subgroup; if the hyphenated form stays, ship help text + docs alignment in the same PR.

nits.issue: The condition `requires_active_task_claim` is asserted as a load trigger but the skill loader's existing condition vocabulary is not referenced.
nits.suggested_fix: Either cite the existing skill loader trigger contract that already supports this predicate, or list the loader-side change required to evaluate it as part of the implementation steps.

nits.issue: Validation says "reject negative durations, fractional seconds, and values greater than the implementation's documented maximum watchdog budget" without naming that maximum.
nits.suggested_fix: Pin the maximum (e.g., `24h`) in `[task.orchestration]` validation rules and surface it in docs.

nits.issue: No explicit test for the full max-runtime managed-stop sequence (request stop ‚Üí grace expire ‚Üí force stop ‚Üí terminal write ‚Üí projection clear) end-to-end.
nits.suggested_fix: Add an integration test under `internal/task` (or scheduler‚Üîtask) covering the full timed_out sequence, plus a race test where the worker sends `CompleteRunLease` after the stop is requested but before the terminal write.

nits.issue: Precedence between `?after_sequence=N` and the `Last-Event-ID` SSE header is unspecified.
nits.suggested_fix: State that `Last-Event-ID` (browser auto-replay) takes precedence on reconnect; first-open uses `?after_sequence` seeded from `latest_event_seq`.

nits.issue: Section is short for a TechSpec that introduces typed columns, new endpoints, new CLI verbs, and bundled skills; the rule (CLAUDE.md "Hard cuts, not bridges") wants explicit enumeration.
nits.suggested_fix: Enumerate every replaced surface: which prose blocks in `coordinator.PromptOverlay` move into `agh-orchestrator`; whether any web polling hook is removed; whether any `metadata_json`/`result_json` legacy parsing is deleted; whether any bridge cursor side-state is migrated/dropped.

summary: The TechSpec's architectural direction (hardening over the existing autonomy substrate, no second queue, projection-not-authority, instructional skills, durable cursors as a shared primitive) is sound and consistent with L-003/L-005/L-006/L-008 and ADR-001..006. However, several safety-critical contracts are underspecified ‚Äî the new task-level terminal operator path against token-fenced active runs, synthetic-run concurrency, the `ClaimNextRun` spawn-circuit filter, the spawn-failure observer/reset call-sites, the public `MaintainCurrentRunID` projection seam, the operator-vs-agent authorization boundary on `/api/tasks/:id/complete|fail`, and a notification cursor primitive shipped without a concrete consumer ‚Äî each of which can produce wrong-behavior tasks if generated as-is. Tighten these before producing `_tasks.md`.
